violations.detail: Cards 5 and 6 are a sacrifice/gain mirror pair. If a CP system 'refuses requests to maintain consistency' during a partition, then an AP system must do the opposite (serve stale data to remain available). Knowing one trivially gives you the other. Should merge or drop one.

Cards 7 and 8 form a pattern where knowing MongoDB is CP and that there are two classification options (CP/AP) makes Cassandra's AP classification more guessable. While not a strict mirror (there are more than two databases), combined with cards 5/6 establishing the CP/AP dichotomy, these reinforce deducibility. Consider merging or reframing one to test a non-obvious aspect.

Of 11 cards: cards 4, 9 are why/misconception (~18%), cards 5, 6 are how (~18%), cards 1, 2, 3, 10, 11 are definitional (~45%), cards 7, 8 are application (~18%). The why/how percentage is borderline acceptable if you count 5 and 6, but the set is still heavier on definitions than the ~30% target for definitions. Could use one more application or reasoning card.

score_justification: Strong card set overall. Good atomicity, backs are concise, no yes/no questions, no enumeration, no example-asking, good tagging, proper cloze usage. Main issues are mirror-deducible pairs (cards 5/6 and to a lesser extent 7/8) and slightly definition-heavy distribution.
